issues.description: The new `_count_diff_changes` function is a near-duplicate of the existing `_git_diff_stats` defined just below it (lines 138-165). Both functions shell out to `git diff --stat <base>..HEAD`, parse the summary line, and return a `(lines_changed, files_changed)` tuple. Two functions with nearly identical purpose but diverging parsing logic, parameter names (`repo_path`/`base_ref` vs `worktree`/`base_commit`), and error handling will inevitably drift and confuse future maintainers.
issues.suggested_fix: Delete `_count_diff_changes` entirely. If a caller needs this functionality, use the existing `_git_diff_stats`. If there is a reason the new variant is needed (e.g. different arg form), justify it in a docstring and factor out the shared parsing — do not ship two parallel implementations.

issues.description: `_count_diff_changes` has zero call sites. `collect_metrics` (line 102) still calls `_git_diff_stats`, and no other file in the repo references the new symbol. Shipping unreferenced code pollutes the module and has no way of being verified in production.
issues.suggested_fix: Either wire it into `collect_metrics` (replacing `_git_diff_stats`) with justification for the change, or drop the function from this diff.

issues.description: Unlike `_git_diff_stats`, the new function has no try/except around the subprocess call or parsing. If `git` is missing, the subprocess errors, `stdout` is malformed, or the summary line contains an unparseable number, an exception will propagate up to `collect_metrics` and fail the entire metrics collection for an eval run. `_git_diff_stats` deliberately swallows errors and returns `(0, 0)` — the new variant violates that contract silently.
issues.suggested_fix: Wrap the body in `try/except Exception: return 0, 0` to match the existing failure contract, or (better) delete the function per the duplication finding.

issues.description: `files_changed = max(0, len(lines) - 1)` counts output lines minus one and assumes the last line is always the summary and every preceding line is a single-file entry. This breaks if git adds a rename block, a binary-file marker, or if output is truncated (e.g., `...`). The existing `_git_diff_stats` uses a regex against the summary line itself (`r'(\d+) files? changed'`), which is robust to these cases.
issues.suggested_fix: Parse the summary line with the same regex used by `_git_diff_stats` instead of counting lines, or reuse `_git_diff_stats` directly.

issues.description: No unit test is added for `_count_diff_changes`. Given that it re-implements existing parsing logic, a parity test against `_git_diff_stats` would at least prove the duplication is behaviorally equivalent; without one, neither function is verified to produce the same result for edge cases (empty diff, binary files, renames, `.gitattributes`-ignored paths).
issues.suggested_fix: If the function is kept, add a parity test using a temporary git repo that creates several commits and asserts both functions return identical tuples. Prefer deleting the new function to avoid the need for duplicated coverage.

issues.description: The new function uses single-quoted strings (`'git'`, `'diff'`, `'--stat'`) and double-space indentation conventions that diverge from the rest of the file's single-line subprocess-exec style. Minor, but inconsistent with the sibling `_git_diff_stats` right below it in the same module.
issues.suggested_fix: Match the existing formatting in `_git_diff_stats` (double-quoted strings in this codebase are used elsewhere; or at minimum keep arg formatting consistent with the neighbor function).

comprehensive_reviewer.summary: The diff adds a new private async helper `_count_diff_changes` that is a functional near-duplicate of the existing `_git_diff_stats` defined immediately below it, with no call sites and no tests. It silently drops the try/except safety net used by `_git_diff_stats`, meaning that if git fails or output is malformed any future caller would crash `collect_metrics` instead of getting `(0, 0)`. Its file-counting logic (`len(lines) - 1`) is also more fragile than the regex-based parser in the sibling function. Recommend deleting the new function entirely; if it must stay, it needs to replace `_git_diff_stats` (not live alongside it), regain error handling, and gain parity tests.
